sections.plain_summary: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition that causes dry, itchy, inflamed skin. It flares and remits over time and is driven by a dysfunctional skin barrier combined with an overactive immune response. It often starts in childhood but can persist into or begin in adulthood. It is not contagious and is not caused by poor hygiene.
sections.detailed_summary: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is one of the most common chronic inflammatory skin diseases worldwide. It is characterised by a defective skin barrier — partly driven by mutations in the filaggrin gene (FLG) — that allows moisture to escape and irritants, allergens, and microbes to penetrate more easily. This triggers an immune response dominated by Th2 cytokines (especially IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31), causing inflammation, itching, and further barrier damage in a self-reinforcing cycle. The itch-scratch cycle is central to the condition: scratching worsens inflammation, which drives more itch. AD is strongly associated with other atopic conditions — asthma, allergic rhinitis, and food allergies — and this cluster is sometimes called the 'atopic march.' Skin colonisation with Staphylococcus aureus is near-universal during flares and actively worsens inflammation. Triggers vary between individuals and include allergens, irritants, sweat, stress, infections, and certain foods. Management focuses on restoring and maintaining the skin barrier with emollients, reducing inflammation during flares with topical or systemic treatments, and identifying and avoiding personal triggers. Newer targeted biologics (e.g., dupilumab) and JAK inhibitors have significantly expanded treatment options for moderate-to-severe disease.

prevalence.summary: Very common — affects roughly 1 in 5 children and up to 1 in 10 adults in high-income countries.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence has risen significantly over the past 50 years, particularly in westernised, urban populations. It is more commonly diagnosed in childhood, but adult-onset AD is more frequent than previously recognised. Rates are lower in rural and low-income settings, supporting the 'hygiene hypothesis.' Figures vary considerably by diagnostic criteria used.

subgroups.name: Extrinsic (allergic) AD
subgroups.description: The most common form (~80% of cases), associated with elevated total IgE and sensitisation to environmental or food allergens.
distinguishing_features: Elevated serum IgE levels
Positive skin-prick or specific IgE tests to allergens
More likely to have comorbid asthma, hay fever, or food allergy
Often onset in early childhood

subgroups.name: Intrinsic (non-allergic) AD
subgroups.description: Minority form (~20%) with normal IgE levels and no identifiable allergen sensitisation, but clinically indistinguishable from extrinsic AD.
distinguishing_features: Normal total and specific IgE
Negative allergy tests
More common in adults and women
May present later in life

subgroups.name: Adult-onset AD
subgroups.description: AD that first appears in adulthood, without a childhood history. Often more severe, treatment-resistant, and associated with psychological burden.
distinguishing_features: No childhood history of eczema
Frequently affects the head, neck, and hands
Higher rates of allergic contact dermatitis as a comorbidity
May be missed or misdiagnosed as contact dermatitis

subgroups.name: Filaggrin-null AD
subgroups.description: Subset with loss-of-function mutations in the FLG gene, conferring a more severe and persistent phenotype.
distinguishing_features: Earlier age of onset
More severe disease with greater transepidermal water loss
Higher risk of asthma and food allergy progression
Ichthyosis vulgaris (dry, fish-scale skin) may co-occur
Detected by genetic testing

subgroups.name: Severe / treatment-refractory AD
subgroups.description: A minority of patients (~5–10%) who do not achieve adequate control with topical therapies and require systemic treatment.
distinguishing_features: Widespread body surface area involvement
Significant sleep disruption and quality-of-life impairment
Frequent secondary infections
Requires specialist management with biologics or systemic immunosuppressants

symptoms.notes: Often worst at night; the primary driver of suffering and sleep disruption. IL-31 is a key cytokine responsible.
Present even between flares due to chronic barrier dysfunction. Skin loses moisture faster than in people without AD.
Distribution varies by age: cheeks and extensor surfaces in infants; flexural creases (elbow, knee) in older children; face, neck, and hands more common in adults.
Indicates active inflammation or secondary bacterial infection (most commonly Staphylococcus aureus).
Results from chronic scratching and rubbing; most common in adults with long-standing disease.
Painful; particularly common on hands, fingers, and behind ears. Can be entry points for infection.
Bacterial (Staph aureus), viral (eczema herpeticum — a medical emergency), or fungal infections occur more readily due to barrier failure.
Nocturnal itch is a hallmark. Chronic sleep deprivation compounds fatigue and mood difficulties.
Extra skin fold or crease under the lower eyelid; a minor diagnostic feature of atopic disease.
Dark circles under the eyes, related to chronic inflammation and rubbing.
Significantly more prevalent in AD than in the general population. Chronic itch and sleep loss are major drivers.
Social withdrawal, stigma, and embarrassment about skin appearance are common, especially in severe disease.
Increased skin creases on the palms; associated with filaggrin mutations.

key_patterns: Clinical diagnosis: itchy skin rash with characteristic distribution and chronic or relapsing course — no single lab test confirms AD
Elevated total serum IgE in extrinsic AD, though not required for diagnosis
Positive specific IgE (RAST) or skin-prick tests to aeroallergens or food allergens in extrinsic subtype
Filaggrin (FLG) gene mutation on genetic testing in a subset of severe/early-onset cases
Skin swab showing heavy Staphylococcus aureus colonisation during flares
Contact allergy patch testing to rule out superimposed allergic contact dermatitis, especially in adults with refractory hand eczema

at_home.name: Symptom and trigger diary
at_home.description: Keep a daily log of skin state (itch intensity 0–10, affected areas, sleep quality) alongside potential triggers: foods eaten, products used, stress levels, sweat exposure, environmental changes, and menstrual cycle phase. After 4–6 weeks, look for patterns between triggers and flare onset.
at_home.safety_notes: This is observational only. Do not attempt food elimination without professional guidance if food allergy (especially IgE-mediated) is suspected in children.

at_home.name: Skin barrier assessment
at_home.description: Assess baseline dryness and barrier function informally: does skin feel tight within minutes of bathing without moisturiser? Does it crack or bleed in dry or cold weather? This helps gauge the degree of barrier impairment and the need for intensive emollient use.
at_home.safety_notes: Not a substitute for clinical assessment.

at_home.name: Elimination and reintroduction (diet)
at_home.description: In adults only, and only if a specific food is strongly suspected as a trigger, trial elimination for 2–4 weeks then deliberate reintroduction to observe skin response. Keep the rest of the routine identical.
at_home.safety_notes: Do not trial multiple eliminations simultaneously. In children, always do this under medical supervision to avoid nutritional deficiencies or triggering IgE-mediated reactions on reintroduction.

clinical.name: Clinical diagnosis using validated criteria
clinical.description: Diagnosed clinically using the Hanifin and Rajka criteria or the UK Working Party Criteria. Requires itchy skin plus 3 or more of: onset before 2 years, flexural involvement, history of dry skin, personal/family atopic history, visible flexural dermatitis. No biopsy or blood test is required.

clinical.name: Specific IgE testing (RAST / ImmunoCAP)
clinical.description: Blood test measuring IgE antibodies to specific allergens (house dust mite, pet dander, pollens, common foods). Identifies sensitisation, not necessarily clinical allergy. Helps in extrinsic AD.

clinical.name: Skin-prick testing (SPT)
clinical.description: Small amounts of allergen extracts are introduced into the skin. Wheal size indicates sensitisation. More immediate and comprehensive than blood IgE testing for aeroallergens.
clinical.availability: Allergy clinic; should not be done during active widespread flares

clinical.name: Patch testing (epicutaneous)
clinical.description: A series of standardised contact allergens are applied to the back under occlusion for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 96 hours. Detects delayed (type IV) contact allergy, which frequently coexists with AD, especially in adults with hand or face eczema.
clinical.availability: Dermatology department; important to exclude in treatment-resistant disease

clinical.name: Total serum IgE
clinical.description: Elevated in extrinsic AD. Helps classify subtype but not required for diagnosis. Useful in monitoring and in workup before biologic therapy.

clinical.name: Filaggrin (FLG) genetic testing
clinical.description: Detects loss-of-function mutations in the FLG gene, identifying a high-risk subset with more severe and persistent disease. Not yet routine in clinical practice but increasingly available.
clinical.availability: Specialist genetics or dermatology centres; limited NHS availability

clinical.name: Skin swab for bacterial culture
clinical.description: Swab of affected skin to identify Staphylococcus aureus colonisation or infection, and to guide antibiotic choice when secondary infection is suspected.

clinical.name: SCORAD / EASI / IGA scoring
clinical.description: Validated severity scoring tools used clinically (SCORing Atopic Dermatitis, Eczema Area and Severity Index, Investigator Global Assessment). Used to guide treatment decisions and monitor response to therapy.
clinical.availability: Used in dermatology clinics and clinical trials

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cells are central to allergic inflammation and itch in AD. MCAS can overlap with or worsen atopic disease, and some treatment-refractory AD patients have features consistent with MCAS.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine drives itch in AD. Some patients with AD report sensitivity to high-histamine foods that appear to worsen their skin, though the evidence base is limited.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety is significantly more prevalent in people with AD. Chronic itch, sleep disruption, and social stigma are key drivers. Stress also directly worsens skin barrier function and flare frequency.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression rates are substantially elevated in moderate-to-severe AD, driven by chronic itch, sleep loss, disfigurement, and functional impairment. Treatment of depression can improve itch perception.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Altered gut microbiome composition in infancy and early childhood is associated with higher AD risk. The gut-skin axis is an active area of research, with gut bacteria influencing systemic immune polarisation toward Th2 responses.

related_conditions.name: Nickel sensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Nickel contact allergy is more common in people with AD. Systemic nickel exposure through diet or jewellery can trigger or worsen eczema flares in sensitised individuals.

related_conditions.name: Salicylate intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Some people with AD report skin worsening with high-salicylate foods or topical products, though evidence for a direct causal role is limited and individual variation is high.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Both AD and eosinophilic disorders (e.g., eosinophilic oesophagitis) share a Th2-driven atopic immune background. They frequently co-occur as part of the atopic march.

related_conditions.name: IgG food sensitivities
related_conditions.explanation: IgG food tests are frequently marketed to eczema patients. However, IgG antibodies reflect food exposure, not disease-causing sensitivity, and IgG testing is not validated for AD diagnosis or management.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic itch in AD can involve central sensitisation, where the nervous system becomes hypersensitised and itch is triggered by normally non-itchy stimuli (alloknesis). This explains why itch can persist even when skin appears clear.

strategies.name: Frequent, generous emollient use
strategies.description: Apply an unperfumed, thick emollient (e.g., white soft paraffin, Epaderm, Cetraben) at least twice daily to the whole body, and immediately after bathing while skin is slightly damp. Emollients restore barrier function, reduce water loss, and reduce the frequency and severity of flares. They are the foundation of all AD management.
strategies.notes: More is better — adult patients can use 500g+ per week. Creams and ointments are more effective than lotions. Avoid products containing fragrances, preservatives, or lanolin if these are personal triggers.

strategies.name: Topical corticosteroids (TCS) for flares
strategies.description: Apply the appropriate-strength corticosteroid cream or ointment to inflamed areas during flares. Mild TCS (1% hydrocortisone) for face and skin folds; moderate-to-potent TCS for body flares. Use for the shortest effective duration, then step down to emollient.
strategies.notes: Steroid phobia is common and leads to under-treatment. When used correctly (right strength, right site, right duration), topical steroids are safe and effective. Overuse on thin skin or face can cause atrophy — follow instructions.

strategies.name: Proactive/maintenance topical therapy
strategies.description: Applying a low-potency TCS or topical calcineurin inhibitor (TCI) to previously affected areas 2–3 times per week even when skin looks clear. This reduces flare frequency significantly compared to reactive-only treatment.
strategies.notes: Particularly useful for people with frequent flares in the same locations. Tacrolimus and pimecrolimus (TCIs) are preferred for sensitive areas (face, genitals) on a long-term basis.

strategies.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (TCIs)
strategies.description: Tacrolimus (Protopic) and pimecrolimus (Elidel) are steroid-free anti-inflammatory options. Suitable for face, neck, and skin folds, or where steroid atrophy is a concern. Can be used long-term.
strategies.notes: May cause initial burning or stinging. A historical black-box warning about theoretical cancer risk is now considered unfounded by major dermatology bodies, but off-label use in children under 2 is not recommended.

strategies.name: Dupilumab (biologic therapy)
strategies.description: Dupilumab (Dupixent) is a monoclonal antibody blocking IL-4 and IL-13 signalling — the core drivers of Th2 inflammation in AD. Subcutaneous injection every 2 weeks. Highly effective for moderate-to-severe AD not controlled by topicals.
strategies.notes: Approved for adolescents and adults; paediatric data growing. Common side effect: conjunctivitis (eye inflammation). Requires specialist prescribing. A major advance for severe AD.

strategies.name: JAK inhibitors (topical and oral)
strategies.description: Topical ruxolitinib and oral baricitinib/upadacitinib/abrocitinib block JAK-STAT signalling to reduce inflammation and itch. Oral JAK inhibitors are particularly fast-acting for itch relief.
strategies.notes: Oral JAK inhibitors carry a class warning for serious infections, malignancy, and cardiovascular events — risk is primarily in older adults with cardiovascular risk factors. Specialist prescribing required. Not suitable for everyone.

strategies.name: Bathing and wet wrap therapy
strategies.description: Lukewarm (not hot) short baths or showers followed immediately by emollient application. Wet wrap therapy — applying emollient/dilute TCS under damp tubular bandages — is highly effective for severe acute flares, particularly in children.
strategies.notes: Wet wraps should be done under nursing or dermatology guidance initially. Avoid soap — use soap substitutes or gentle syndets only.

strategies.name: Identifying and reducing personal triggers
strategies.description: Common triggers include: house dust mite, pet dander, pollen, mould, certain foods (particularly in young children), sweat, wool or synthetic fibres, fragranced products, harsh detergents, low humidity, temperature extremes, and psychological stress. Systematic identification and avoidance reduces flare frequency.
strategies.notes: Triggers are highly individual. Blanket dietary restriction without identified allergy is not recommended and risks nutritional harm, especially in children.

strategies.name: Stress management and psychological support
strategies.description: Psychological stress directly worsens AD via neuroimmune pathways. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), mindfulness-based stress reduction, and habit reversal training (for scratch behaviour) have evidence in AD.
strategies.notes: The itch-scratch cycle has a strong behavioural component. Habit reversal training specifically targets automatic scratching and has RCT evidence.

strategies.name: Environmental allergen reduction (house dust mite)
strategies.description: Use allergen-impermeable mattress and pillow covers, wash bedding weekly at 60°C, reduce soft furnishings and carpets, maintain humidity between 40–50%. HDM is the most common environmental trigger in AD.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger for asthma than AD, but HDM reduction is reasonable for sensitised AD patients and carries no harm.

strategies.name: Bleach baths
strategies.description: Dilute sodium hypochlorite baths (approximately 0.005% — half a teaspoon of 5% household bleach per 4.5L of water, in a full bath) 2–3 times weekly reduce Staphylococcus aureus skin load and can reduce flare severity and frequency.
strategies.notes: Should not sting if properly diluted. Rinse off and apply emollient immediately after. Check product concentration and dilute correctly. Avoid if skin is actively broken or infected.

strategies.name: Antihistamines for itch
strategies.description: Non-sedating antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine) have limited evidence for AD itch specifically (which is not primarily histamine-mediated). Sedating antihistamines (e.g., chlorphenamine) may help short-term with sleep disruption during flares.
strategies.notes: AD itch is predominantly driven by IL-31 and neurological sensitisation, not histamine. Antihistamines are widely used but evidence for itch control in AD specifically is weak.

strategies.name: Probiotics (preventive use in pregnancy/infancy)
strategies.description: Maternal and/or infant probiotic supplementation (particularly Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG) has modest evidence for reducing AD incidence in high-risk infants, potentially by shaping early gut microbiome and immune development.
strategies.notes: Evidence is primarily preventive, not therapeutic. Using probiotics to treat established AD in adults has weak evidence. Most promising in the first 6 months of life in high-risk families.

strategies.name: Vitamin D supplementation
strategies.description: Vitamin D deficiency is associated with worse AD severity. Supplementation (particularly in deficient individuals) may modestly improve skin barrier function and reduce flare frequency.
strategies.notes: Check 25(OH)D levels before supplementing. Effect size is modest. More relevant in those who are genuinely deficient.

things_to_avoid: Fragranced skincare products, soaps, and household cleaners — fragrance is one of the most common contact allergens and irritants in AD
Hot showers or baths — hot water strips the skin barrier and worsens dryness and itch
Wool and rough synthetic fabrics directly against the skin — these are physical irritants that trigger itch
Scratching — damages the skin barrier, worsens inflammation, and increases infection risk; trim nails short and use distraction or cold packs instead
Abrupt discontinuation of topical steroids after prolonged heavy use — this can trigger topical steroid withdrawal (TSW), a controversial but real phenomenon with rebound inflammation
Over-reliance on antibiotics for Staph colonisation without true infection — this drives antibiotic resistance without meaningful benefit
Unnecessary dietary elimination without identified allergy — risks nutritional deficiency and social harm without evidence of benefit in most adult AD
IgG food sensitivity testing to guide dietary changes — IgG tests are not validated for AD and routinely produce false positives leading to unnecessary restriction
Leaving moderate-to-severe disease untreated — chronic uncontrolled inflammation causes lichenification, infection, and significant psychological harm; effective treatments exist
Using topical steroids of incorrect potency for the site — potent steroids on the face or genitals cause atrophy; under-treating with too-weak steroids prolongs flares

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no cure for atopic dermatitis. Management is ongoing, indefinite, and requires sustained daily effort even when skin looks clear.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients — especially parents of affected children — frequently seek a definitive fix, whether dietary, environmental, or medical. The idea that this is lifelong work feels demoralising.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who accept this typically shift from crisis-only treatment to proactive daily routines (emollients, maintenance therapy) and see a dramatic reduction in flare frequency and severity over 6–12 months. Life becomes less governed by the condition, not more.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Steroid phobia causes more harm than appropriate topical steroid use. Many people are suffering unnecessarily because they are under-treating flares out of fear.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Online communities, alternative health spaces, and sometimes well-meaning but poorly informed healthcare providers amplify fears about steroid side effects, leading to emollient-only approaches that cannot control active inflammation.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who learn to use the right strength steroid at the right site for the right duration typically get flares under control within days rather than weeks, breaking the itch-scratch-inflammation cycle and reducing overall lifetime steroid use compared to dragged-out under-treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most adult food elimination diets for eczema — including gluten-free, dairy-free, and low-histamine — do not work for the majority of people and can cause nutritional harm.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Diet feels empowering and controllable. The appeal of 'healing from the inside out' is strong, especially when standard treatments feel insufficient or chemical.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Adults who focus resources on skin barrier repair, trigger identification, and appropriate medical treatment typically see better skin outcomes than those spending the same energy on dietary restriction without confirmed allergy or sensitivity.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Topical steroid withdrawal (TSW) is real for a small subset of patients, but it is also frequently self-diagnosed online by people who actually have undertreated AD, leading them to abandon effective treatment.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: TSW communities are large and vocal, and the stories are compelling. It is genuinely difficult to distinguish TSW from a severe rebound flare of undertreated AD.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who work with a dermatologist to accurately characterise their situation — rather than self-diagnosing via social media — are more likely to receive the right treatment and avoid months of unnecessary suffering from untreated inflammation.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Psychological distress is not just a consequence of eczema — it actively worsens the skin through neuroimmune pathways. Treating the mind is treating the skin.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: This can feel dismissive ('it's all in your head'). Patients often resist psychological framing of a visibly physical condition.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who engage with CBT, habit reversal training, or stress reduction alongside medical treatment typically report lower itch scores, fewer flares, and better quality of life than those who treat the skin alone.

key_levers.lever: Daily emollient routine, applied proactively not reactively
key_levers.why_it_matters: Barrier repair is the most fundamental intervention in AD. Consistent emollient use reduces transepidermal water loss, limits allergen and microbe penetration, and directly reduces flare frequency. A single daily application is not enough for most people.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose a fragrance-free, thick emollient (ointment or cream, not lotion). Apply to the whole body within 3 minutes of patting dry after bathing, and again at least once more during the day. Use at minimum 250g/week for adults.
key_levers.timeline: Improvement in skin texture within 2–4 weeks; reduction in flare frequency over 2–3 months

key_levers.lever: Proactive topical therapy on previously affected sites
key_levers.why_it_matters: Applying a low-potency TCS or TCI twice weekly to areas that historically flare — even when the skin looks clear — prevents subclinical inflammation from becoming a full flare. This is the single most evidence-backed strategy for reducing flare frequency.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify your 2–4 most frequently affected areas. After a flare has cleared, apply your prescribed topical treatment to those areas twice weekly going forward.
key_levers.timeline: Significant reduction in flare frequency typically seen within 3–6 months

key_levers.lever: Systematic trigger identification and personalised avoidance
key_levers.why_it_matters: Everyone's AD has a different trigger profile. Random avoidance wastes effort. A structured diary approach identifies which triggers (if any) are genuinely contributing for a specific individual.
key_levers.how_to_start: Keep a daily log for 4–6 weeks, scoring itch and skin state daily alongside food, products used, stress levels, and environment. Look for consistent patterns. Test hypotheses one at a time.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful patterns usually emerge within 4–8 weeks

key_levers.why_it_matters: Scratching is largely automatic and habitual. It directly damages the skin barrier, triggers further inflammation, and makes itch worse. Habit reversal training — a specific behavioural technique — has RCT evidence in AD.
key_levers.how_to_start: When you notice the urge to scratch, apply a competing response: cold pack, firm pressure with palm (not fingernails), or squeezing an object. Keep nails short. Consider self-help resources on habit reversal for eczema.
key_levers.timeline: Noticeable reduction in automatic scratching within 4–8 weeks with consistent practice

key_levers.lever: Accessing effective systemic treatment for moderate-to-severe disease
key_levers.why_it_matters: Topical treatment alone is insufficient for a meaningful proportion of AD patients. Modern biologics (dupilumab) and JAK inhibitors are transformative for severe AD. Under-treatment causes cumulative skin damage and profound psychological harm.
key_levers.how_to_start: If your eczema is consistently affecting more than ~10% of body surface area, causing significant sleep disruption, or not responding to topical treatment, ask for a dermatology referral to discuss systemic options.
key_levers.timeline: Dupilumab: significant improvement typically within 4–16 weeks. Oral JAK inhibitors: itch often improves within days

key_levers.why_it_matters: Nocturnal itch and sleep disruption are the primary quality-of-life burdens in AD. Poor sleep increases inflammatory cytokines, raises cortisol, and directly worsens skin. Sleep is both a consequence and a driver of disease activity.
key_levers.how_to_start: Apply emollient and any flare treatment before bed. Keep the bedroom cool and humidity stable. Consider cotton gloves or sleepsuits to reduce unconscious scratching. If sleep is severely disrupted, discuss options with a doctor.
key_levers.timeline: Improvement tracks skin improvement; expect better sleep within 2–4 weeks of good flare control

common_mistakes: Treating AD reactively only — applying treatment when flares start rather than maintaining a daily preventive routine. Flares become self-reinforcing without baseline management.
Stopping emollient use when skin clears — the barrier dysfunction is chronic and underlying, not just present during visible flares.
Under-treating flares with too-weak steroids, leading to prolonged low-grade inflammation that is harder to control than an acute flare treated promptly and adequately.
Pursuing food elimination diets without confirmed allergy — this consumes time, money, and social energy while the skin continues to suffer from inadequate topical management.
Abandoning effective medical treatment to pursue elimination diets, supplements, or alternative therapies as a primary approach, delaying control of a treatable condition.
Waiting for a 'perfect' trigger identification before treating skin — barrier repair and inflammation control should happen in parallel with trigger investigation, not after.
Not moisturising after swimming or exercise — sweat and pool water are significant barrier disruptors and trigger itch if not quickly removed and replaced with emollient.

Assuming eczema is simply dry skin requiring any moisturiser — many over-the-counter moisturisers contain fragrance, preservatives, or active ingredients (like urea at high concentrations) that irritate AD-affected skin.

specialists.role: Primary specialist for AD diagnosis, severity classification, patch testing to exclude contact allergy, prescribing of topical regimens, and access to systemic therapies including biologics and JAK inhibitors.
specialists.notes: Essential for moderate-to-severe disease or for disease not controlled by GP-prescribed topicals. Long waiting times in NHS settings; private consultation may accelerate access.

specialists.role: Specialist in allergy testing (skin-prick testing, specific IgE), food allergy assessment, and the management of the atopic triad (AD, asthma, hay fever) as an integrated whole.
specialists.notes: Particularly valuable where food allergy is suspected as a trigger, especially in children. Important for assessing risk of anaphylaxis before dietary reintroduction.

specialists.role: First point of contact for diagnosis and initial management. Can prescribe emollients, topical steroids, and topical calcineurin inhibitors. Manages referrals and coordinates care.
specialists.notes: May have variable knowledge of newer therapies; a proactive patient asking specifically about dupilumab or dermatology referral often gets better outcomes.

specialists.role: Specialist in AD in children, including infants with severe disease, wet wrap therapy guidance, and paediatric biologic prescribing.
specialists.notes: Essential for children with severe, treatment-refractory, or complicated AD. Paediatric AD management differs significantly from adult management.

specialists.role: Provides intensive education on emollient technique, wet wrap therapy, topical regimen adherence, and psychological support. Often the most practically useful contact for day-to-day management.
specialists.notes: Available in many NHS dermatology departments and charity-run eczema clinics. Often underutilised by patients.

specialists.role: Provides cognitive behavioural therapy and habit reversal training, both of which have evidence in reducing itch, scratch behaviour, and the psychological burden of chronic AD.
specialists.notes: Itch-focused CBT and habit reversal training are not widely available but can sometimes be accessed via psychodermatology services or private practice.

patient_communities.name: r/eczema
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community of adults with AD. Useful for peer support, product recommendations, and shared experience. Contains some misinformation (particularly around TSW and unproven treatments) — evaluate advice critically.

patient_communities.name: National Eczema Association (NEA) Community
patient_communities.notes: US-based patient organisation with evidence-based resources, a product directory of approved products, advocacy work, and an online patient forum. Reliable, well-moderated.

patient_communities.name: Eczema Outreach Support (EOS)
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity specifically for families of children with eczema. Practical support, peer connection, and helpline.

patient_communities.name: National Eczema Society
patient_communities.notes: UK's leading eczema charity. Provides a nurse-staffed helpline, patient guides, treatment information, and advocacy. Excellent starting point for UK patients.

patient_communities.name: Eczema Support Australia
patient_communities.notes: Active peer support group for Australian patients. Good for navigating the Australian healthcare system and finding specialist recommendations.

learning_resources.title: National Eczema Society — Patient Resources
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, evidence-based patient guides covering diagnosis, treatment, triggers, and specific subtypes. Written in plain language by clinicians. Covers both children and adults. The best single starting point for UK patients.

learning_resources.title: National Eczema Association — Treatment Guide
learning_resources.description: Clear, regularly updated overview of treatment options from topicals to biologics. Includes a Seal of Acceptance product database for suitable skincare. Well suited to adults newly navigating AD.

learning_resources.title: Eczema: Coping with Atopic Dermatitis
learning_resources.author_or_source: Peter Elias and colleagues (various self-management guides)
learning_resources.description: Several evidence-based self-management books exist for AD. Look for books written or endorsed by dermatologists covering barrier repair, trigger management, and psychological coping. Ask your dermatologist or the NES for current recommendations.

learning_resources.title: DermNet — Atopic Dermatitis
learning_resources.description: Clinically accurate, detailed overview of AD including pathophysiology, clinical features, investigations, and treatment. More technical than patient leaflets — useful for people who want to understand the science.

learning_resources.title: The Itch Podcast
learning_resources.description: Interviews with dermatologists and patients covering the latest research, treatment options, and lived experience of AD. Particularly good for keeping up with developments in biologic and JAK inhibitor therapies.

learning_resources.title: NICE Guideline NG190: Atopic Eczema in Under 12s
learning_resources.author_or_source: NICE (National Institute for Health and Care Excellence)
learning_resources.description: UK clinical guideline for managing eczema in children. Useful for parents wanting to understand what standard care should look like and how to advocate effectively within the NHS.

learning_resources.title: TREAT Registry / BIODAY Registry — AD Research Updates
learning_resources.author_or_source: European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology (EADV)
learning_resources.description: For those interested in the latest clinical evidence and real-world data on AD biologics and JAK inhibitors, the TREAT and BIODAY registries publish outcome data. Access through dermatology publications or the EADV website.

sections.relevance_signal: **Persistent itchy, dry, inflamed skin** in elbow creases, knees, or face — often dating back to childhood.
